_schema.purpose: Registry of City of Berkeley Annual Comprehensive Financial Reports (ACFRs). The ACFR is the only city financial document subjected to independent external audit — all other reports are produced by city staff or the internal City Auditor. The external auditor's opinion is the most authoritative signal available about whether the city's financial statements are fairly presented. An unmodified (clean) opinion is the baseline; anything else is a high-severity event. The ACFR also contains the authoritative figures for pension liability, OPEB, fund balances, and debt — these numbers are the ground truth that council members are accountable to.
_schema.distinction_from_audit_findings: audit_findings.json tracks performance and compliance audits by the internal City Auditor (a city employee). acfr_reports.json tracks the externally audited financial statements — a wholly independent process. The two can and should cross-reference each other: the City Auditor's financial condition report (financial_condition_2026) drew its pension and fund balance figures from ACFR data.
_schema.berkeley_fiscal_year: Berkeley's fiscal year ends June 30. FY2024 = July 1, 2023 – June 30, 2024. ACFRs are typically published 5-6 months after year-end.

status_values.pending_review: ACFR obtained but key figures not yet extracted
status_values.extracted: Key figures and opinion captured in registry and/or source extract
status_values.scored: Figures incorporated into pipeline scoring or incidents

fields.audit_firm: Name of the independent external audit firm
fields.audit_opinion: unmodified | qualified | adverse | disclaimer — unmodified is the only acceptable baseline
fields.emphasis_of_matter: Any emphasis of matter or other matter paragraphs from the auditor's report — these flag issues short of a qualified opinion
fields.going_concern: Boolean — has the auditor raised substantial doubt about going-concern status
fields.net_pension_liability: Net pension liability per GASB 68 schedule (dollars)
fields.opeb_liability: Other post-employment benefits liability per GASB 75 (dollars)
fields.key_facts: Notable items from the MD&A or financial statements
fields.scoring_relevance: How figures in this ACFR connect to council accountability
fields.source_url: URL to PDF on berkeleyca.gov — fetch directly; no local copy needed
fields.source_extract: Path to JSON extract with verbatim MD&A language and detailed figures (populated after manual text extraction)

acfr_fy2023.net_pension_liability_note: INCREASED $235.9M (+53.3%) from $442.5M in FY2022 — largest single-year jump in the three-year set. Primarily an actuarial/GASB 68 recognition event.
key_facts: Net pension liability jumped $235.9M (+53.3%) in one year — from $442.5M to $678.4M. Council received this disclosure with the ACFR.
GF fund balance grew $10.4M to $158.5M — a strong revenue year that may have masked the pension trajectory.
ARPA federal funds used to balance FY2022 and FY2023; remaining ARPA deferred to FY2024, creating a known revenue cliff.
Property transfer tax collapsed from $39.7M (FY2022) to $22.0M (FY2023) — -44.6% — as interest rates rose. Revenue structure is volatile.
Section 115 Trust growing ($4.7M contribution) — council was still building the fund that FY2025/FY2026 would draw down.
Reserve target policy: 30% of GF revenues by 2027. By FY2025 council lowered target to 20-30%, moving the goalposts.
Public safety overtime over budget by $4.1M — first year of what became a three-year pattern.
trend_signals: Pension liability: $442.5M (FY2022) → $678.4M (FY2023) → $723.8M (FY2024) → $686.4M (FY2025) — a large recognition event followed by continued growth before modest improvement.
GF fund balance: $158.5M (FY2023) → $153.0M (FY2024) → ~$155M (FY2025, estimated) — growing reserves reversed in FY2024.
Section 115 Trust: $19.1M (FY2023) → $26.5M (FY2024, +$7.4M) → $30.7M (FY2025, -$3M drawdown) — inflection toward drawdown began FY2025.
Public safety overtime over budget: $4.1M (FY2023) → $8.2M (FY2024) → $9.3M (FY2025) — the problem doubled and continued growing.
acfr_fy2023.scoring_relevance: The baseline year for three-year trend analysis. The $236M pension liability jump establishes that council was aware pension costs were accelerating. The FY2023 reserve target (30% of GF revenues by 2027) was later quietly lowered to 20-30% — the council moved the goalposts rather than meeting the original target. The 5-year GF fund balance trend (included in extract) shows FY2023 was the peak; decline began FY2024.

acfr_fy2024.net_pension_liability_note: INCREASED $45.4M from $678.4M in FY2023 — pension liability was growing. City Auditor's April 2026 report cited $694.8M, which is the FY2025 figure.
acfr_fy2024.section_115_trust_note: Trust GREW $7.4M (from $19.1M) in FY2024 — city was contributing, not drawing down.
key_facts: GF expenditures exceeded revenues by $21.1M before transfers — operating gap required reserves and fund balance draws.
GF fund balance decreased $5.4M (from $158.5M to $153.0M) — reserves declining.
Net pension liability increased $45.4M to $723.8M — pension costs were still growing in FY2024.
Section 115 Trust grew $7.4M to $26.5M — city was still contributing; drawdown began in FY2025.
Public safety overtime exceeded budget by $8.2M — same structural pattern as FY2025 ($9.3M over).
Transmittal discloses FY2025-2026 biennial budget one-time measures: Section 115 draws, workers' comp non-funding, salary savings — council received this disclosure when adopting the biennial budget.
Non-sustainability language present: 'these strategies lead to a balanced biennial budget, they are not sustainable' — but less direct than FY2025's explicit 'structural deficit' language.
trend_signals: Pension liability growing: +$45.4M in FY2024 vs. -$37.4M in FY2025 — FY2025 improvement may not persist.
Section 115 Trust inflection: contributing in FY2024, drawing down in FY2025 — the transition happened with full council knowledge.
Public safety overtime: $8.2M over in FY2024, $9.3M over in FY2025 — 13% year-over-year growth, no structural fix proposed.
acfr_fy2024.scoring_relevance: FY2024 is the prior year establishing the baseline trajectory. The City Auditor's April 2026 report cited $694.8M net pension liability — this is the FY2025 figure (ACFR: $723.8M in FY2024 → $686.4M in FY2025). The FY2024 ACFR discloses the same one-time measures the City Auditor later flagged, confirming council had advance disclosure.

bond_ratings.moodys_note: Upgraded from Aa2 shortly before FY2025. Ratings assess default risk, not operational sustainability — both a strong rating and a structural deficit can coexist.

acfr_fy2025.opeb_liability_note: 50% drop from $83.4M in FY2024 — likely actuarial assumption change, not actual benefit reduction. Requires note review.
key_facts: Director of Finance explicitly states in transmittal letter: 'The General Fund faces a structural deficit that cannot be resolved through one-time solutions alone.'
FY2026 budget balanced using: $6.0M Section 115 withdrawal, $5.2M workers' comp transfer, $6.2M IT fund transfer, 44.4 vacant positions unfunded — all one-time measures, larger than FY2025.
Unmodified audit opinion — external auditor found no material misstatements; no emphasis of matter paragraph regarding reserve withdrawals.
Bond rating upgraded to Aa1 (Moody's) despite structural deficit — rating agencies assess debt service capacity, not operational sustainability.
Public safety overtime exceeded budget by $9.3M — staffing shortages forcing mandatory overtime.
Net pension liability decreased $37.4M to $686.4M — an improvement but still dominant liability.
OPEB liability dropped 50% in one year ($77.5M to $38.7M) — likely actuarial assumption change.
Policy contradiction: fiscal policy #5 requires one-time revenue for one-time expenditures; Section 115 Trust is being drawn down for operating pension expenses.
Reserve policy amended July 2025 to target 20-30% of GF revenues — at $285.8M revenue that's $57.2-$85.7M; current $58.3M is at the floor.
trend_signals: One-time balancing measures grew from FY2025 ($4.7M workers' comp + $3M Section 115) to FY2026 ($5.2M workers' comp + $6.0M Section 115) — escalating, not stabilizing.
GF expenditures grew 14.5% ($241.8M → $276.9M) year over year — significantly outpacing revenue growth of 9.2%.
Health and welfare GF spending up 36.2% in one year; economic development up 40.1%.
acfr_fy2025.scoring_relevance: The Director of Finance's explicit structural deficit acknowledgment in an externally audited document is the highest-confidence version of the City Auditor's April 2026 finding. Any council member claiming ignorance of the deficit after receiving this ACFR is directly contradicted by this document. The FY2026 one-time measures listed here are larger than FY2025 — the council adopted these measures with full knowledge of the structural problem.
